  5. End/Clear: Once the checkpoint is finished, the journal entries are no longer needed.

Why this works

If the system crashes during step 2, there is no "Commit" record. On reboot, the filesystem sees an incomplete transaction and ignores it. The main filesystem remains in its old, consistent state.

If the system crashes during step 4, the "Commit" record exists. On reboot, the filesystem sees the commit and replays the blocks from the journal into the main filesystem. The operation is completed as intended.

3. Implementing the Journal in C

Your Journal needs a simple API that wraps your existing block I/O.

c
// Wrapper for metadata modification
void journal_log_metadata_block(uint32_t physical_block_num) {
    uint8_t buffer[BLOCK_SIZE];
    device_read_block(dev, physical_block_num, buffer);
    
    // 1. Write the target block number so we know where this goes during replay
    // 2. Write the 4KB of data into the journal's 'tail'
    // 3. Advance the journal tail
}

void journal_commit(uint32_t tx_id) {
    // Write the Commit Record
    // MANDATORY: Call fdatasync() here!
    // We must ensure the commit record hits the platter before we 
    // touch the main filesystem. This is a [[EXPLAIN:write-barrier|Write Barrier]].
}

The Checkpoint

A "Checkpoint" is the act of cleaning the journal. Because the journal has a fixed size, you will eventually run out of space.

  • Periodically (or when the journal is 80% full), you must ensure all journaled changes have been written to the primary data structures.
  • Use fsync() to ensure the primary structures are safe.
  • Update the head pointer in the bitfs_journal_header_t to effectively "delete" the old transactions.

4. Recovery: The "Resurrection" Algorithm

When your filesystem mounts, the first thing it should do isn't reading the root directory—it's checking the journal.

The Recovery Algorithm:

  1. Read the bitfs_journal_header_t from the journal start.
  2. Start at the head and walk towards the tail.
  3. For each transaction:
    • Validate the checksum. If it fails, the transaction is "torn" (corrupted) and must be discarded.
    • Check for a Commit Record.
    • If Committed: Iterate through the logged blocks in the transaction and write them to their physical_block_num in the main filesystem. This is called Replaying.
    • If Not Committed: Stop. Discard this and all subsequent transactions.
  4. Once the scan is complete, perform a checkpoint() to zero out the journal.

🔑 Foundation: Idempotency is the property of an operation that can be applied multiple times without changing the result beyond the initial application.

1. What it IS > In systems programming, an idempotent operation is one where f(x) is the same as f(f(x)). For example, setting a bit to 1 is idempotent (doing it twice doesn't change the bit to anything else). Incrementing a counter by 1 is not idempotent (doing it twice results in a different value). > > 2. Why you need it right now > During crash recovery, you might be replaying a journal entry that was already partially written to the disk before the crash. If your replay logic isn't idempotent, you might double-apply a change (like incrementing a link count), leading to a corrupted filesystem. > > 3. Key Insight > The "Light Switch" vs. "The Toggle": A light switch with "ON" and "OFF" positions is idempotent. No matter how many times you hit "ON," the light stays on. A single "Toggle" button is not idempotent—hitting it twice leaves the light in the original state. In recovery, you always want "ON/OFF" logic.

Three-Level View: A Journaled mkdir

Let's see what happens at each level when you run mkdir /new_dir:

  1. Application (Level 1): Calls mkdir().
  2. OS/Kernel (Level 2):
    • Your code starts a Transaction.
    • It reads the Inode Bitmap (Block 2). It logs Block 2 to the Journal.
    • It reads the Inode Table block (Block 10) where the new Inode will live. It logs Block 10 to the Journal.
    • It logs the Parent Directory's Data Block (Block 500) where the new entry will go.
    • It writes a Commit Record to the Journal.
    • (Crash Happens Here!)
  3. Hardware (Level 3):
    • On reboot, the recovery code sees the Commit Record in the Journal.
    • It "replays" Blocks 2, 10, and 500 from the Journal into the main filesystem.
    • Even though the crash happened before the "real" blocks were updated, the Journal ensures the mkdir is completed.

The Hardware Soul: Write Amplification and Barriers

Journaling introduces a massive hardware penalty: Write Amplification.

Every metadata write now happens twice—once in the journal and once in the main filesystem. For small files, this can double the I/O load. This is the price we pay for immortality.

To minimize this, we use Write Barriers. In C, this is fdatasync() or open with O_DIRECT | O_SYNC. This tells the disk: "Do not reorder writes across this boundary."

If your disk ignores barriers (common in cheap consumer SSDs), your journal is useless. Systems engineers must often choose hardware based on whether it has Power-Loss Protection (PLP)—on-board capacitors that provide just enough energy to flush the volatile cache to the NAND if the power is cut.

Knowledge Cascade: Surface Connections

  1. Databases (WAL): Every modern database (PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, SQLite) uses this exact technique. In SQLite, it's literally called "WAL Mode." It's faster for databases because it turns random B-Tree updates into sequential log appends.
  2. Distributed Systems (Raft/Paxos): In a distributed database (like Etcd or CockroachDB), the "Journal" isn't just on one disk—it's replicated across 3 or 5 servers. A transaction is only "Committed" when it has been written to the journal of a majority of the servers.
  3. SSD FTL (Flash Translation Layer): Internally, your SSD is doing its own journaling! Because NAND flash cannot be overwritten (it must be erased in large blocks), the SSD controller keeps a log of where it moved your data. This is why SSDs are so much faster than HDDs but also more complex to recover when they fail.
  4. Event Sourcing: In high-level application architecture, "Event Sourcing" is just journaling for business logic. Instead of storing the "Current State" of a bank account, you store a journal of every transaction. The current balance is just a "replay" of the journal.

Design Decisions: Metadata vs. Full Journaling

Mode What is Journaled Pros Cons
Metadata ✓ Inodes, Bitmaps, Dirs Fast, protects FS integrity. File data can still be corrupted.
Full Metadata + File Data Total consistency. 2x Write Amplification on everything.
Ordered Metadata only (strict order) Prevents "garbage data" in files. Default for ext4.

We chose Metadata Journaling because it is the standard for general-purpose filesystems. It ensures that after a crash, the filesystem is always "valid," even if a file you were currently writing contains a mix of old and new data.

Acceptance Criteria for Milestone 6

Your journaling and recovery implementation must satisfy these requirements:

  1. Journal Persistence: The journal header and transaction records must be stored in the dedicated journal region defined in the superblock.
  2. Atomic Transactions:
    • Every metadata-modifying function (create, unlink, mkdir, etc.) must be wrapped in a transaction.
    • A transaction is only valid if a complete Commit Record is found.
  3. Checksum Validation: Each transaction must include a checksum. The recovery logic must discard any transaction where the checksum doesn't match (detecting torn writes).
  4. Idempotent Replay: The recovery logic must be safe to run multiple times. Replaying a transaction that was already partially or fully applied to the main filesystem must not cause corruption.
  5. Crash Recovery Success:
    • Test Case: Run a script that creates 1000 files. While it's running, kill -9 the filesystem process.
    • On remount, the recovery code must run automatically.
    • fsck (or your equivalent verification tool) must show zero orphaned inodes and zero bitmap inconsistencies.
  6. Journal Wrap-around: The journal must correctly handle the circular buffer logic, overwriting the oldest checkpointed transactions when space is needed.
  7. Write Barriers: Your commit logic must use fdatasync() or an equivalent to ensure the journal is durable before the main filesystem is modified.

Congratulations, Architect.

You have built a complete, journaled, inode-based filesystem from the raw blocks up. You have moved from the "infinite sea of bytes" to the rigid, immortal reality of the 4KB grid. Your BitFS is now ready for the real world.

System Overview

TDD

A production-grade, FUSE-compatible filesystem that bridges the 4KB hardware block reality with a transactional, hierarchical metadata model. Focus is on memory-aligned structures, O(1) inode lookups, and crash-resilient metadata journaling.

MODULE CHARTER: Block Layer and mkfs (filesystem-m1)

The Block Layer and mkfs module serves as the physical-to-logical bridge for the BitFS filesystem. It abstracts a raw host-operating-system file into a linear array of fixed-size 4096-byte (4KB) blocks. This module is responsible for the initial "formatting" of the disk image—calculating the filesystem geometry, partitioning the blocks into functional regions (Superblock, Bitmaps, Inode Table, Journal, and Data Blocks), and persisting the foundational metadata.

What it DOES:

  • Provides a strict 4KB block I/O interface, forbidding sub-block or unaligned access.
  • Defines the binary format of the Superblock, the authoritative source of filesystem geometry.
  • Implements a BitmapAllocator to track the lifecycle of every data and metadata block.
  • Provides the mkfs.bitfs utility to initialize a fresh, consistent filesystem state from a zeroed file.

What it does NOT do:

  • Manage Inode metadata (Milestone 2).
  • Handle directory traversals or path resolution (Milestone 3).
  • Provide crash consistency via journaling (Milestone 6).

Invariants:

  • Every I/O operation must be exactly BLOCK_SIZE bytes and start at an offset divisible by BLOCK_SIZE.
  • The Superblock must always reside at Physical Block 0.
  • Bitmaps must represent every block in the disk image; no block may exist without a corresponding bit.

FILE STRUCTURE

The implementation follows a kernel-style C structure, prioritizing clear separation between the hardware abstraction (Block Device) and the formatting logic (mkfs).

  1. include/bitfs/blocks.h: Block device structure and I/O function signatures.
  2. include/bitfs/layout.h: On-disk structures (Superblock, Bitmap constants) with explicit packing.
  3. src/block_device.c: Implementation of the file-backed I/O wrapper.
  4. src/bitmap.c: Implementation of bit-level manipulation and searching.
  5. src/mkfs.c: The standalone formatting utility.
  6. tests/test_block_io.c: Unit tests for block-aligned reads/writes and boundary conditions.

COMPLETE DATA MODEL

1. The Block Device (Memory Context)

The bitfs_block_device_t represents the open state of the disk image. It is a runtime-only structure.

c
typedef struct {
    int fd;                 // File descriptor to the disk image
    uint32_t total_blocks;  // Total size in 4KB units
    char* path;             // Path to the image file
} bitfs_block_device_t;

2. The Superblock (On-Disk Layout)

The Superblock is the "God Object" of BitFS. It must be exactly 4096 bytes to occupy Block 0, though only the first few hundred bytes are used.

Offset Type Name Description
0x00 uint32_t magic Magic Number (0x42495442)
0x04 uint32_t version Filesystem version (currently 1)
0x08 uint32_t block_size Fixed at 4096
0x0C uint32_t total_blocks Total blocks in the device
0x10 uint32_t inodes_count Total capacity of Inode Table
0x14 uint32_t free_blocks Number of currently free data blocks
0x18 uint32_t free_inodes Number of currently free inodes
0x1C uint32_t bm_block_start Starting block of the Block Bitmap
0x20 uint32_t bm_inode_start Starting block of the Inode Bitmap
0x24 uint32_t inode_table_start Starting block of the Inode Table
0x28 uint32_t journal_start Starting block of the Journal
0x2C uint32_t data_start Starting block of Data Region
0x30 uint8_t[4048] padding Reserved for expansion / Block alignment

C Definition:

c
#define BITFS_MAGIC 0x42495442
#define BLOCK_SIZE 4096

typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
    uint32_t magic;
    uint32_t version;
    uint32_t block_size;
    uint32_t total_blocks;
    uint32_t inodes_count;
    uint32_t free_blocks;
    uint32_t free_inodes;

    uint32_t bm_block_start;
    uint32_t bm_inode_start;
    uint32_t inode_table_start;
    uint32_t journal_start;
    uint32_t data_start;

    uint8_t padding[4048]; 
} bitfs_superblock_t;

INTERFACE CONTRACTS

1. Block Device API

Function: bitfs_block_device_t* bitfs_device_open(const char* path)

  • Input: Path to a file (should be pre-allocated to the desired size).
  • Output: Context pointer or NULL on failure.
  • Constraints: Verifies file existence and permissions. Calculates total_blocks via fstat.

Function: int bitfs_device_read(bitfs_block_device_t* dev, uint32_t block_num, void* buf)

  • Input: Device context, block index, destination buffer (must be at least 4KB).
  • Output: 0 on success, BITFS_ERR_IO on failure, BITFS_ERR_BOUNDS if block_num >= dev->total_blocks.
  • Logic: Performs lseek(dev->fd, block_num * BLOCK_SIZE, SEEK_SET) followed by read.

Function: int bitfs_device_write(bitfs_block_device_t* dev, uint32_t block_num, const void* buf)

  • Input: Device context, block index, source buffer.
  • Output: 0 on success, BITFS_ERR_IO on failure.
  • Hardware Soul: Must ensure the write is flushed to the host OS cache using fdatasync if atomicity is required by the caller.

2. Bitmap API

Function: int bitfs_bitmap_set(uint8_t* bitmap_block, uint32_t bit_index, bool value)

  • Logic: byte = bit_index / 8; bit = bit_index % 8;.
  • Invariants: bit_index must be < (BLOCK_SIZE * 8).

ALGORITHM SPECIFICATION: mkfs Geometry Calculation

The mkfs tool must calculate the size of each region based on the total available space.

Inputs: total_blocks, requested_inodes. Procedure:

  1. Superblock: Reserve Block 0.
  2. Block Bitmap: Size = ceil(total_blocks / (BLOCK_SIZE * 8)) blocks.
  3. Inode Bitmap: Size = ceil(requested_inodes / (BLOCK_SIZE * 8)) blocks.
  4. Inode Table: Size = ceil((requested_inodes * INODE_SIZE) / BLOCK_SIZE) blocks.
  5. Journal: Fixed size (e.g., 512 blocks) or 5% of total disk.
  6. Data Region: total_blocks - (all previous regions).
  7. Validation: Ensure Data Region > 0. If not, reduce requested_inodes.

Bitmap Search Optimization (Hardware Soul)

To find a free block (bit 0), we process the bitmap in uint64_t chunks.

  1. Load 8 bytes (64 bits) into a CPU register.
  2. Compare with 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF.
  3. If not equal, use __builtin_ctzll(~val) (Count Trailing Zeros) to find the exact bit index in $O(1)$ within that word.
  4. This reduces cache-line fetches by 64x compared to bit-by-bit checking.

Error Detected By Recovery User-Visible?

E_IO_FAILURE read() / write() syscalls Log failure, return BITFS_ERR_IO. Yes
E_CORRUPT_SB device_open Verify Magic Number. Fail if mismatch. Yes (Invalid FS)
E_BITMAP_FULL alloc_block Return 0 (NULL block pointer). Yes (Disk Full)
E_TORN_SB_WRITE mkfs fsync the block after writing. If failure, mkfs fails. Yes

Phase 1: The Raw Grid (2-3 Hours)

Goal: Create a 4KB-aligned I/O abstraction.

  1. Implement bitfs_device_open and bitfs_device_close.
  2. Implement bitfs_device_read and bitfs_device_write.
  3. Checkpoint: Create a 1MB file. Write a known pattern to Block 5. Read Block 5 back. Verify with memcmp.

Phase 2: Serialization and Bitmaps (3-4 Hours)

Goal: Define the on-disk structures and bit-manipulation logic.

  1. Define bitfs_superblock_t in layout.h.
  2. Implement bitmap helper functions (set, get, find_first_zero).
  3. Checkpoint: Write a test that flips every 7th bit in a 4KB block, writes it to disk, reads it back, and verifies the bit-pattern integrity.

Phase 3: The mkfs Utility (5-7 Hours)

Goal: A functional formatter.

  1. Calculate offsets for all regions based on a 100MB target image size.
  2. Initialize the Superblock and write it to Block 0.

  4. Mark the Superblock and Metadata blocks as "Used" in the Block Bitmap.

TEST SPECIFICATION

1. Block Boundary Test

  • Action: Attempt to read Block total_blocks + 1.
  • Expected: Return BITFS_ERR_BOUNDS.
  • Action: Attempt to write Block 0.
  • Expected: Success (Superblock is writeable).

2. Bitmap Capacity Test

  • Action: Create a 1-block bitmap. Fill all bits but the last one. Call find_first_zero.
  • Expected: Returns index 32767.
  • Action: Fill the last bit. Call find_first_zero.
  • Expected: Returns 0 or error (No free blocks).

3. Alignment Test

  • Action: Pass a buffer that is not 4KB-aligned to device_write.
  • Expected: Technically handled by OS, but verify that our logic doesn't truncate or misplace data. (Note: In high-perf systems, we would use O_DIRECT which requires the buffer to be page-aligned).

CRASH RECOVERY (Module 1 Scope)

While journaling is implemented in Milestone 6, Milestone 1 must handle Dirty Mount Detection.

  • The Superblock should contain a state flag.
  • mkfs sets state = BITFS_STATE_CLEAN.
  • When mounting, the driver sets state = BITFS_STATE_DIRTY.
  • On graceful unmount, the driver sets state = BITFS_STATE_CLEAN.
  • If mount detects BITFS_STATE_DIRTY, it refuses to mount without an fsck.

MODULE CHARTER: Inode Management (filesystem-m2)

The Inode Management module implements the core logical identity of the BitFS filesystem. It transitions the system from raw, anonymous blocks into a structured metadata system where files are represented by Index Nodes (Inodes). This module defines the physical on-disk layout of the 128-byte inode, manages the Inode Table, and implements the multi-level pointer tree required to support variable-sized files ranging from 1 byte to 4GB.

What it DOES:

  • Defines the authoritative binary structure for file metadata (mode, size, links, timestamps).
  • Implements the Pointer Tree translation logic (bmap), mapping logical file offsets to physical disk blocks via direct, single-indirect, and double-indirect pointers.
  • Manages the lifecycle of inodes: allocation from the inode bitmap, serialization to the Inode Table, and recursive deallocation of all data and indirect blocks.
  • Provides support for Sparse Files by interpreting null pointers as zero-filled "holes."

What it does NOT do:

  • Resolve human-readable filenames (handled by Milestone 3: Directory Operations).
  • Handle the FUSE callback layer (handled by Milestone 5).
  • Ensure atomic multi-block updates (handled by Milestone 6: Journaling).

Invariants:

  • Every inode must be exactly 128 bytes, ensuring 32 inodes fit perfectly into a single 4KB block.
  • Block pointers in the inode structure must always point to a valid data region block or be 0 (NULL).
  • The nlink (link count) must be greater than zero for any active inode; an inode with nlink == 0 is eligible for immediate deallocation.

FILE STRUCTURE

The implementation follows a strict separation between header definitions, core inode logic, and the pointer-mapping engine.

  1. include/bitfs/inode.h: Struct definitions, bitmask constants, and inode-level API.
  2. src/inode.c: Inode Table RMW logic and lifecycle management (alloc/free).
  3. src/bmap.c: The pointer-traversal engine (logical-to-physical mapping).
  4. tests/test_inode.c: Unit tests for indirection limits and sparse file handling.

COMPLETE DATA MODEL

1. The Inode Structure (bitfs_inode_t)

The inode is the "Hardware Soul" of the file. To minimize cache misses, related metadata is grouped together. Since a cache line is typically 64 bytes, a single inode spans exactly two cache lines.

Byte-Level Layout Table:

Offset Size Type Name Description
0x00 2 uint16_t mode File type (Dir/Reg) and POSIX permissions.
0x02 2 uint16_t nlink Number of hard links.
0x04 4 uint32_t uid User ID of the owner.
0x08 4 uint32_t gid Group ID of the owner.
0x0C 4 uint32_t size Current file size in bytes.
0x10 4 uint32_t atime Last access time (Unix Epoch).
0x14 4 uint32_t mtime Last modification time (Unix Epoch).
0x18 4 uint32_t ctime Last status change time (Unix Epoch).
0x1C 48 uint32_t[12] direct Pointers to the first 12 data blocks.
0x4C 4 uint32_t indirect Pointer to a block of 1024 pointers.
0x50 4 uint32_t db_indirect Pointer to a block of single-indirect blocks.
0x54 44 uint8_t[44] reserved Padding to reach 128 bytes exactly.

C Implementation:

c
#include <stdint.h>
#include <time.h>

#define BITFS_DIRECT_PTRS 12
#define BITFS_INODE_SIZE 128
#define BITFS_PTRS_PER_BLOCK (BLOCK_SIZE / sizeof(uint32_t)) // 1024

typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
    uint16_t mode;
    uint16_t nlink;
    uint32_t uid;
    uint32_t gid;
    uint32_t size;
    uint32_t atime;
    uint32_t mtime;
    uint32_t ctime;
    
    uint32_t direct[BITFS_DIRECT_PTRS]; // 0-11: First 48KB
    uint32_t indirect;                 // 12: Next 4MB
    uint32_t db_indirect;              // 13: Next 4GB
    
    uint8_t reserved[44];
} bitfs_inode_t;

Figure 1: Inode Memory Layout. 0x00-0x3F (First Cache Line: Metadata + first 8 pointers). 0x40-0x7F (Second Cache Line: Remaining pointers + indirection + padding).

INTERFACE CONTRACTS

1. Inode Table Access

Function: int bitfs_inode_read(bitfs_block_device_t* dev, uint32_t ino, bitfs_inode_t* out_inode)

  • Input: Device context, Inode ID.
  • Output: 0 on success, E_INVALID_INODE if ino is out of range.
  • Logic:
    1. uint32_t inodes_per_block = BLOCK_SIZE / BITFS_INODE_SIZE;
    2. uint32_t block_num = sb->inode_table_start + (ino / inodes_per_block);
    3. uint32_t offset = (ino % inodes_per_block) * BITFS_INODE_SIZE;
    4. Read 4KB block into temporary buffer.
    5. memcpy(out_inode, buffer + offset, BITFS_INODE_SIZE);

Function: int bitfs_inode_write(bitfs_block_device_t* dev, uint32_t ino, const bitfs_inode_t* inode)

  • Input: Device context, Inode ID, source struct.
  • Logic: Performs the Read-Modify-Write cycle. You must read the 4KB block containing the inode, overwrite only the 128 bytes for this Inode ID, and write the full block back.

2. Allocation Lifecycle

Function: int bitfs_inode_alloc(bitfs_block_device_t* dev, uint16_t mode, uint32_t* out_ino)

  • Logic:
    1. Scan bm_inode for the first zero bit.
    2. Mark bit as 1 and update free_inodes in the Superblock.
    3. Initialize all metadata fields to current time and zeros.
    4. Persist to disk.

Function: int bitfs_inode_free(bitfs_block_device_t* dev, uint32_t ino)

  • Constraints: Must only be called when nlink == 0.
  • Logic:
    1. Call bitfs_truncate(inode, 0) to recursively free all data and indirect blocks.
    2. Clear the bit in bm_inode.

ALGORITHM SPECIFICATION: The bmap Engine

The bmap function (Block Map) is the most critical logic for file I/O. It translates a logical file block index (e.g., "Give me the 500th block of this file") into a physical disk block number.

Procedure: uint32_t bitfs_bmap(bitfs_inode_t* inode, uint32_t logical_idx)

  1. Direct Range (0 to 11):

    • If logical_idx < 12: Return inode->direct[logical_idx].

  2. Single Indirect Range (12 to 1035):

    • uint32_t rel_idx = logical_idx - 12;
    • If rel_idx < 1024:
      • If inode->indirect == 0: Return 0 (Sparse Hole).
      • Read physical block inode->indirect into a buffer.
      • Return ((uint32_t*)buffer)[rel_idx].

  3. Double Indirect Range (1036 to 1,049,611):

    • uint32_t rel_idx = logical_idx - 12 - 1024;
    • uint32_t first_level_idx = rel_idx / 1024;
    • uint32_t second_level_idx = rel_idx % 1024;
    • If inode->db_indirect == 0: Return 0.
    • Read inode->db_indirect (First level: block of pointers to blocks of pointers).
    • Get uint32_t mid_block_ptr = buffer[first_level_idx].
    • If mid_block_ptr == 0: Return 0.
    • Read mid_block_ptr (Second level: block of pointers to data).
    • Return buffer[second_level_idx].

Figure 2: Pointer Tree Architecture. Visualizing the jump from 48KB (Direct) to 4MB (Indirect) to 4GB (Double).

IMPLEMENTATION SEQUENCE

Phase 1: Inode struct design and serialization (3-4 Hours)

Goal: Successfully read and write the root inode (#0).

  1. Define the bitfs_inode_t struct with packed attribute.
  2. Implement bitfs_inode_read and bitfs_inode_write.
  3. Checkpoint: Manually set Inode 0 size to 1234 in mkfs. Run a test program that reads Inode 0 and asserts size == 1234. Use hexdump to verify the byte offset in the image file.

Phase 2: Multi-level pointer traversal (bmap) (4-6 Hours)

Goal: Support large files up to 4GB.

  1. Implement bitfs_bmap with support for all three tiers.
  2. Implement a "Setter" version of bmap that allocates blocks if they are missing (needed for writing).
  3. Checkpoint: Create an inode. Assign a block to direct[0], indirect[5], and db_indirect[10][20]. Call bmap for those logical indices and verify the physical blocks match.

Phase 3: Inode allocation and recursive deallocation (3-4 Hours)

Goal: Clean up storage when files are deleted.

  1. Implement bitfs_inode_alloc using the inode bitmap.
  2. Implement recursive deallocation logic.
    • Crucial: If db_indirect is freed, you must first free all 1024 data-pointer blocks, then the 1 block-pointer block, then the inode's pointer.
  3. Checkpoint: Allocate an inode, grow it to 10MB (using indirect blocks), then free it. Assert that the free_blocks count in the Superblock returns to the original value exactly.

CONCURRENCY SPECIFICATION

In a multi-threaded environment (like FUSE), concurrent access to the Inode Table is a primary source of race conditions.

Locking Strategy:

  1. Global Inode Bitmap Lock: A mutex must be held during bitfs_inode_alloc and bitfs_inode_free.
  2. Per-Inode Mutex: Each bitfs_inode_t structure should be protected by a mutex when loaded into memory. This prevents two threads from performing the RMW cycle on the same inode simultaneously, which would cause one update to be lost.
c
// Recommended in-memory inode wrapper for FUSE
typedef struct {
    bitfs_inode_t v;       // The actual disk data
    uint32_t ino;          // ID
    pthread_mutex_t lock;  // Protects 'v'
} bitfs_inode_handle_t;

MODULE CHARTER: Directory Operations (filesystem-m3)

The Directory Operations module transforms the BitFS from a flat collection of numbered inodes into a navigable, hierarchical tree. It implements the "Folder Illusion" by treating directories as special files whose data blocks contain a serialized table of variable-length records. This module is the primary bridge between user-facing string paths (e.g., /var/log/syslog) and internal inode identifiers.

What it DOES:

  • Defines the Variable-Length Directory Entry (dirent) format, optimized for space efficiency and fast sequential scanning.
  • Implements the Path Resolver (namei), which iteratively tokenizes strings and traverses the inode tree starting from the root (Inode #1).
  • Manages the lifecycle of directory entries: adding entries (with gap-filling logic), removing entries (with record-merging logic), and ensuring name uniqueness.
  • Enforces Link Count Integrity, specifically handling the nlink increments required for the .. (dot-dot) entries in subdirectories.
  • Implements mkdir, rmdir, link (hard link), and unlink (delete) logic.

What it does NOT do:

  • Handle file data I/O (handled by Milestone 4).
  • Manage the FUSE kernel interface directly (handled by Milestone 5).
  • Provide atomicity for multi-block directory updates (handled by Milestone 6).

Invariants:

  • Every directory MUST contain . (pointing to self) and .. (pointing to parent) as its first two entries.
  • The root directory's .. entry MUST point to itself (Inode #1).
  • No two entries in the same directory data block may have the same name.
  • A directory cannot be removed (rmdir) unless it is "empty" (contains only . and ..).

FILE STRUCTURE

The directory logic is organized to separate the low-level record manipulation from high-level path traversal.

  1. include/bitfs/dir.h: dirent struct definitions and directory-level constants.
  2. src/dir_entry.c: Logic for searching, adding, and removing records within 4KB blocks.
  3. src/path.c: The namei implementation (path tokenization and traversal).
  4. src/dir_ops.c: High-level implementations of mkdir, rmdir, link, and unlink.
  5. tests/test_dir.c: Comprehensive tests for long filenames, deep nesting, and link counting.

COMPLETE DATA MODEL

1. The Directory Entry (bitfs_dirent_t)

BitFS uses variable-length records to minimize internal fragmentation. Each entry is aligned to a 4-byte boundary to ensure efficient CPU access to the inode_num.

Byte-Level Layout Table:

Offset Size (Bytes) Type Name Description
0x00 4 uint32_t inode_num Target Inode ID (0 indicates a deleted/invalid entry).
0x04 2 uint16_t rec_len Distance in bytes to the start of the NEXT entry.
0x06 1 uint8_t name_len Length of the filename string (max 255).
0x07 1 uint8_t file_type Type hint: 1 (Regular), 2 (Directory).
0x08 name_len char[] name Filename (not null-terminated on disk).
Varies 0-3 uint8_t[] padding Padding to align the NEXT rec_len to 4 bytes.

C Implementation:

c
#include <stdint.h>

#define BITFS_NAME_MAX 255
#define BITFS_FT_REG   1
#define BITFS_FT_DIR   2

// Macros for alignment
#define BITFS_DIR_PAD 4
#define BITFS_DIR_ROUND(len) (((len) + BITFS_DIR_PAD - 1) & ~(BITFS_DIR_PAD - 1))

typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
    uint32_t inode_num;    // 4 bytes
    uint16_t rec_len;      // 2 bytes
    uint8_t  name_len;     // 1 byte
    uint8_t  file_type;    // 1 byte
    char     name[];       // Flexible array member
} bitfs_dirent_t;

// The minimum size of an entry for a name of length N:
// size = BITFS_DIR_ROUND(8 + N)

Visualizing the variable-length stream: [Ino:5, Rec:12, Name:"foo"] [Ino:9, Rec:16, Name:"bardata"] [Ino:0, Rec:24, Deleted...]

INTERFACE CONTRACTS

1. Path Resolution

Function: int bitfs_resolve_path(const char* path, uint32_t* out_ino)

  • Input: Absolute path string (e.g., /usr/bin/ls).
  • Output: 0 on success, error code on failure.
  • Errors: E_NOT_FOUND (component missing), E_NOT_DIR (component in middle of path is a file).

2. Directory Modification

Function: int bitfs_dir_add(uint32_t parent_ino, const char* name, uint32_t target_ino, uint8_t type)

  • Input: Parent directory Inode, name of new entry, target Inode ID, type hint.
  • Constraints: name must be <= 255 chars. Name must be unique in parent_ino.
  • Logic: Calls bitfs_bmap to find/allocate directory data blocks and invokes the gap-filling algorithm.

Function: int bitfs_dir_remove(uint32_t parent_ino, const char* name)

  • Logic: Finds the entry, sets inode_num = 0, and merges the rec_len with the previous valid record to avoid fragmentation.

3. High-Level Ops

Function: int bitfs_mkdir(uint32_t parent_ino, const char* name, uint16_t mode)

  • Requirements:
    1. Check if name exists in parent_ino.
    2. Allocate new Inode child_ino (Type: DIR).
    3. Allocate 1 data block for child_ino.
    4. Initialize child_ino data block with . (target: child_ino) and .. (target: parent_ino).
    5. Add entry name to parent_ino.
    6. Increment nlink of parent_ino (because of child_ino's .. link).

ALGORITHM SPECIFICATION: Directory Entry Management

1. The Gap-Filling Algorithm (add_entry)

To keep directories compact, we reuse space from deleted entries before appending.

Procedure:

  1. Calculate required_len = BITFS_DIR_ROUND(8 + new_name_len).
  2. Iterate through directory data blocks (via bmap):
    • For each block:
      • ptr = block_start.
      • While ptr < block_end:
        • dirent = (bitfs_dirent_t*)ptr.
        • actual_min_len = BITFS_DIR_ROUND(8 + dirent->name_len).
        • If dirent->inode_num == 0 AND dirent->rec_len >= required_len:
          • Found a deleted gap. Use this record. (Keep rec_len as is). Return.
        • If dirent->rec_len - actual_min_len >= required_len:
          • Found trailing space in an active record.
          • Create new record at ptr + actual_min_len.
          • new_dirent->rec_len = dirent->rec_len - actual_min_len.
          • dirent->rec_len = actual_min_len.
          • Return.
        • ptr += dirent->rec_len.
  3. If no space found in existing blocks:
    • Allocate new data block via balloc.
    • Initialize block with one giant empty record: inode_num = 0, rec_len = 4096.
    • Repeat search (it will now succeed).

2. The namei Resolution (Path to Inode)

Procedure:

  1. curr_ino = BITFS_ROOT_INO (usually 1).
  2. Tokenize path by /.
  3. For each token:
    • Read curr_ino metadata. Verify S_ISDIR(mode).
    • Iterate through data blocks of curr_ino.
    • Search for dirent where dirent->name == token.
    • If not found: Return ENOENT.
    • curr_ino = dirent->inode_num.
  4. After last token: *out_ino = curr_ino.

Figure 1: namei sequence diagram. Step-by-step traversal from Root -> Block Read -> Dirent Match -> Child Inode.

CONCURRENCY & LOCKING

Directory operations are highly prone to race conditions (e.g., two processes creating files in the same folder simultaneously).

Locking Protocol:

  1. Parent-Lock Rule: To modify any directory, you MUST hold a pthread_mutex_t associated with the Parent Inode.
  2. Order of Acquisition: When moving/renaming, always acquire locks in increasing order of Inode ID to prevent deadlocks.
  3. Atomic Bitmaps: The block/inode bitmaps have their own internal locks (from M1/M2), but the directory data modification must be atomic relative to other directory searches.

ERROR HANDLING MATRIX

Error Detected By Recovery User-Visible?
E_NOT_FOUND namei / lookup Return ENOENT to VFS. Yes
E_EXISTS mkdir / create Return EEXIST (cannot create duplicate). Yes

E_DIR_NOT_EMPTY rmdir Scan directory; if any entry other than . or .. exists, return error. Yes

Goal: Create helpers to navigate the variable-length stream inside a 4KB block.

  2. Implement bitfs_dir_search_in_block(block_ptr, name) returning a dirent pointer.

Phase 2: Path Resolution (namei) (4-5 Hours)

Goal: Successfully find an inode by its full path.

  1. Implement tokenization (be careful with strtok as it is not thread-safe; use strtok_r).
  2. Implement the iterative bitfs_resolve_path.
  3. Checkpoint: Manually create a structure /test/foo.txt in your mkfs or a test script. Call resolve_path("/test/foo.txt") and verify it returns the correct Inode ID.

Phase 3: Directory Modification (3-5 Hours)

Goal: Support mkdir, link, and unlink.

  1. Implement the add_entry logic with gap-filling.
  2. Implement bitfs_mkdir (don't forget the .. link count increment on the parent!).
  3. Implement bitfs_unlink (decrement nlink and merge rec_len).
  4. Checkpoint: Run a test that creates 50 files with different name lengths. Use ls -f (raw order) to verify that when you delete file #25, file #51 is placed into the vacated gap.

TEST SPECIFICATION

1. The Alignment Boundary Test

  • Action: Add an entry with a name of 1 byte ("a"). Add an entry with a name of 4 bytes ("abcd").
  • Expected: The rec_len for "a" should be 12 (8 bytes header + 1 char + 3 bytes padding). The rec_len for "abcd" should also be 12 (8 bytes header + 4 chars + 0 padding).

2. The .. Link Count Test

  • Action: Check nlink of root (Inode 1). Perform mkdir("/subdir").
  • Expected: nlink of root should increase by 1.
  • Reason: The entry /subdir/.. is a new hard link to the root directory.

3. The Maximum Filename Test

  • Action: Attempt to mkdir a directory with a 256-character name.
  • Expected: Return ENAMETOOLONG.
  • Action: Create a 255-character name.
  • Expected: Success.

Operation Target How to Measure
Lookup (100 files) < 1ms time for bitfs_resolve_path.
Add Entry (Gap Reuse) $O(N)$ blocks Ensure it scans all blocks for a gap before allocating.

HARDWARE SOUL: CACHE LINE IMPACT

In BitFS, a 4KB directory block holds approximately 80-150 entries depending on name length.

  • A CPU cache line is 64 bytes.
  • A standard dirent (e.g., name length 12) is 24 bytes.
  • Efficiency: We can fit ~2.5 entries per cache line.

Optimization Advice: When searching for a name, we only touch the rec_len and name_len of non-matching entries. To minimize cache pollution, avoid reading the inode_num until the name comparison matches. This ensures we stay in the "hot" part of the cache line as we skip past irrelevant files.

MODULE CHARTER: File Read/Write Operations (filesystem-m4)

The File Read/Write Operations module implements the data-plane of the BitFS filesystem. It serves as the critical translation layer between the user-centric "stream of bytes" abstraction and the hardware-centric "fixed-size block" reality. This module is responsible for the intricate logic of mapping arbitrary file offsets and lengths onto physical 4KB disk blocks, managing the Read-Modify-Write (RMW) cycles required for unaligned sub-block updates, and ensuring that file metadata (size, timestamps) remains synchronized with the physical data on disk.

What it DOES:

  • Implements the "Slicing Logic" that decomposes a large I/O request into a sequence of block-aligned operations.
  • Manages the Bounce Buffer (a 4KB staging area) used to perform partial-block modifications.
  • Implements Block Allocation on Write, growing files dynamically as data is appended or written to sparse holes.
  • Supports Sparse Files, ensuring that reads from unallocated blocks return zeros and writes to high offsets do not physically allocate intermediate blocks.
  • Provides the truncate operation to reclaim or reserve disk space by modifying the file's logical end-point.

What it does NOT do:

  • Handle path resolution or directory entry management (handled by Milestone 3).
  • Directly interact with the FUSE kernel module (handled by Milestone 5).
  • Guarantee crash consistency (handled by Milestone 6).

Invariants:

  • A file's physical block count must always be sufficient to cover its logical size, except in the case of sparse holes where pointers are explicitly 0.
  • All data I/O must pass through the bmap engine to resolve logical-to-physical addressing.
  • Metadata (mtime, ctime, size) must be updated and persisted to the Inode Table upon the completion of any successful write or truncate operation.

FILE STRUCTURE

The implementation follows a modular structure, separating the core I/O logic from the buffer management.

  1. include/bitfs/file.h: Public API for file I/O and truncation.
  2. src/file_io.c: Implementation of slicing logic and RMW cycles.
  3. tests/test_file_ops.c: Functional tests for alignment, sparse files, and throughput.

COMPLETE DATA MODEL

This module primarily operates on the bitfs_inode_t structure defined in Milestone 2. However, it introduces the concept of an in-memory I/O Context to manage buffers during the RMW process.

1. The I/O Context (Internal)

Used during a single read or write call to track progress across the 4KB grid.

c
typedef struct {
    uint32_t logical_block;   // Current block index in the file (0, 1, 2...)
    uint32_t block_offset;    // Byte offset within the current block (0-4095)
    uint32_t bytes_to_copy;   // Calculated slice size for the current block
    uint8_t* bounce_buffer;   // 4KB memory-aligned staging area
} bitfs_io_context_t;

2. File Metadata Alignment (Hardware Soul)

To ensure maximum performance on modern hardware (SSDs and HDDs with 4KB physical sectors), this module prioritizes Aligned I/O.

Requirement Value Reason
Buffer Alignment 4096 bytes Allows for potential use of O_DIRECT or zero-copy DMA.
Slicing Unit 4096 bytes Matches the OS Page Size and Filesystem Block Size.
RMW Penalty 1 Read + 1 Write Incurred whenever a write does not start and end on a 4KB boundary.

Figure 1: The Slicing Window. A user request for 10KB at offset 2KB is sliced into: [Block 0: 2KB tail] [Block 1: 4KB full] [Block 2: 4KB full].

INTERFACE CONTRACTS

1. File Read

Function: ssize_t bitfs_read(uint32_t ino, void *buf, size_t count, off_t offset)

  • Input: Inode ID, destination buffer, byte count, file offset.
  • Output: Number of bytes read (may be less than count if EOF reached) or negative error code.
  • Constraints:
    • If offset >= inode.size, return 0 (EOF).
    • If offset + count > inode.size, truncate count to read only until inode.size.
  • Edge Case: Reading from a "hole" (physical block pointer is 0) must return a buffer of zeros without performing disk I/O.

2. File Write

Function: ssize_t bitfs_write(uint32_t ino, const void *buf, size_t count, off_t offset)

  • Input: Inode ID, source buffer, byte count, file offset.
  • Output: Number of bytes written or negative error code (-ENOSPC if disk full).
  • Logic:
    • Must allocate blocks via balloc if the write targets an unallocated block.
    • If offset + count > inode.size, update the inode's size field.
  • Concurrency: Must acquire the Inode-level lock before modification to prevent race conditions during RMW.

3. File Truncation

Function: int bitfs_truncate(uint32_t ino, off_t length)

  • Input: Inode ID, new desired length.
  • Logic:
    • Shrink: Iterate from new_block_count to old_block_count and free blocks using bfree. Update indirect pointers to 0.
    • Grow: Simply update inode.size. This creates a sparse hole at the end of the file.

ALGORITHM SPECIFICATION

1. The "Slicing" Read Algorithm

Procedure:

  1. Load bitfs_inode_t from disk.
  2. Initialize bytes_read = 0.
  3. Allocate a 4KB bounce_buffer.
  4. While bytes_read < count:
    • l_block = (offset + bytes_read) / 4096.
    • block_off = (offset + bytes_read) % 4096.
    • chunk_size = min(4096 - block_off, count - bytes_read).
    • p_block = bmap(inode, l_block).
    • If p_block == 0:
      • memset(buf + bytes_read, 0, chunk_size).
    • Else:
      • bitfs_device_read(dev, p_block, bounce_buffer).
      • memcpy(buf + bytes_read, bounce_buffer + block_off, chunk_size).
    • bytes_read += chunk_size.
  5. Update inode.atime = current_time().
  6. Persist inode. Return bytes_read.

2. The Read-Modify-Write (RMW) Write Algorithm

This is essential for preserving data in partially overwritten blocks.

Procedure:

  1. Lock the Inode.
  2. While bytes_written < count:
    • l_block = (offset + bytes_written) / 4096.
    • block_off = (offset + bytes_written) % 4096.
    • chunk_size = min(4096 - block_off, count - bytes_written).
    • p_block = bmap(inode, l_block).
    • If p_block == 0:
      • p_block = balloc(dev). If failure, return -ENOSPC.
      • bmap_link(inode, l_block, p_block).
      • memset(bounce_buffer, 0, 4096). (Initialize new block)
    • Else if chunk_size < 4096:
      • bitfs_device_read(dev, p_block, bounce_buffer). (Read existing data)
    • Modify: memcpy(bounce_buffer + block_off, buf + bytes_written, chunk_size).
    • Write: bitfs_device_write(dev, p_block, bounce_buffer).
    • bytes_written += chunk_size.
  3. If offset + bytes_written > inode.size: inode.size = offset + bytes_written.
  4. Update mtime, ctime.
  5. Persist Inode. Unlock. Return bytes_written.

Figure 2: RMW Logic Flow. Decision tree for whether to Read before writing based on block alignment.

ERROR HANDLING MATRIX

Error Detected By Recovery User-Visible?
E_DISK_FULL balloc Stop write, return bytes_written so far or -ENOSPC. Yes
E_IO_ERROR device_read Abort operation, return -EIO. Yes
E_VAL_TOO_LARGE bitfs_write If offset + count > 4GB (Double-Indirect limit), return -EFBIG. Yes
E_INVALID_OFFSET bitfs_read If offset < 0, return -EINVAL. Yes

IMPLEMENTATION SEQUENCE

Phase 1: Read Slicing Logic (3-4 Hours)

Goal: Correctly handle unaligned reads and EOF conditions.

  1. Implement the loop that calculates l_block and chunk_size.
  2. Integrate with bmap (from M2) to handle sparse holes.
  3. Checkpoint: Create a file with "ABCDE" in Block 0 and "FGHIJ" in Block 1 using mkfs. Run bitfs_read(ino, buf, 4, 3). Verify buf contains "DE" + "FG".

Phase 2: Write and RMW Logic (4-6 Hours)

Goal: Support file growth and sub-block updates without data corruption.

  1. Implement the block allocation trigger within the write loop.
  2. Implement the RMW buffer logic for partial blocks.
  3. Implement metadata updates (size, mtime).
  4. Checkpoint: Write "Hello" at offset 0, then write "World" at offset 2. Read back offset 0. Verify the string is "HeWorld". Use hexdump to confirm only 1 block is allocated.

Phase 3: Truncation and Sparse Expansion (3-4 Hours)

Goal: Reclaim space and support massive logical sizes.

  1. Implement bitfs_truncate for shrinking (recursive bfree).
  2. Implement bitfs_truncate for growing (size-only update).
  3. Checkpoint: Create a 10MB file. Truncate to 1KB. Verify free_blocks count increases. Truncate to 1GB. Verify size is correct but free_blocks does not change.

CONCURRENCY SPECIFICATION

To support multi-threaded I/O from FUSE, BitFS must enforce per-inode serialization for writes.

  1. The Inode Lock: A pthread_mutex_t should be part of the in-memory inode handle.
  2. Read/Write Competition:
    • Multiple threads may Read the same inode simultaneously (Shared Lock).
    • Only one thread may Write or Truncate a specific inode at a time (Exclusive Lock).
  3. Bitmap Deadlocks: Ensure that the block bitmap lock is acquired after the Inode lock but before the physical block write.
c
int bitfs_write(...) {
    inode_lock(ino);
    // ... all allocation and writing logic ...
    inode_unlock(ino);
}

TEST SPECIFICATION

1. The "Straddle" Test

  • Action: Write 8192 bytes starting at offset 2048.
  • Expected:
    • Block 0 (Physical A) is read, modified (last 2048 bytes), and written.
    • Block 1 (Physical B) is allocated and written fully.
    • Block 2 (Physical C) is allocated, initialized with 0s, modified (first 2048 bytes), and written.

2. The Sparse Read/Write Test

  • Action: Open new file. truncate(ino, 1024*1024) (1MB). write(ino, "data", 4, 512*1024) (middle).
  • Expected:
    • Only 1 data block is allocated.
    • bmap for block 0 returns 0.
    • bmap for block 128 (middle) returns a valid physical block.
    • Reading block 0 returns 4096 zeros.

3. The Full Disk Test

  • Action: Repeatedly write to a file until balloc returns 0.
  • Expected: bitfs_write returns -ENOSPC. The file's size is updated to reflect only the successfully written bytes.

Operation Target How to Measure
Sequential Write > 80% raw disk dd into the mounted BitFS; compare with dd to host file.
Random Write (4K) < 2ms (HDD) / < 100μs (SSD) Measure latency of aligned 4K writes.
Read Amplification 1:1 (Aligned) / 2:1 (Unaligned) Count device_read_block calls relative to request size.

HARDWARE SOUL: THE BOUNCE BUFFER

In this module, the bounce_buffer is the primary interface with the hardware.

  • Cache Line Efficiency: When memcpy-ing from the user buffer to the bounce buffer, the CPU utilizes L1/L2 write-combining buffers.
  • Alignment: The bounce buffer should be allocated using posix_memalign to a 4096-byte boundary. This ensures that the underlying host OS can perform Direct I/O without an additional copy if the host filesystem supports it.
  • TLB Pressure: Since we reuse the same 4KB bounce buffer for the duration of a loop, we minimize TLB misses by keeping the memory "hot" in the CPU's address translation cache.

MODULE CHARTER: FUSE Integration (filesystem-m5)

The FUSE Integration module serves as the primary gateway between the internal BitFS logic and the Linux Kernel's Virtual File System (VFS). By implementing the fuse_operations callback interface, this module allows the BitFS image to be mounted as a standard directory, accessible by any POSIX-compliant utility (e.g., ls, cat, cp, mkdir). It effectively translates high-level kernel requests—which identify files by string paths—into the inode-based and block-based operations developed in Milestones 1 through 4.

What it DOES:

  • Implements the FUSE dispatcher logic, mapping kernel-space syscalls to internal BitFS functions.
  • Provides string-path-to-inode resolution for every incoming request using the namei logic from Milestone 3.
  • Manages the lifecycle of the filesystem process, including mounting, unmounting, and clean resource disposal.
  • Enforces system-wide concurrency constraints using a locking model to ensure metadata integrity during multi-threaded kernel access.
  • Translates BitFS error codes into standard POSIX errno values expected by the kernel.

What it does NOT do:

  • Handle low-level block I/O (delegated to bitfs_block_device).
  • Perform direct on-disk metadata manipulation (delegated to inode and dir modules).
  • Provide crash consistency (handled by the Journaling module in Milestone 6).

Invariants:

  • Every FUSE callback must return a standard negative errno (e.g., -ENOENT) on failure.
  • A global filesystem lock must be held during any operation that modifies bitmaps or the inode table to prevent race conditions.
  • The root directory (/) must always map to Inode #1.

FILE STRUCTURE

The integration is contained within a dedicated FUSE wrapper layer that bridges the project's library logic with the libfuse runtime.

  1. include/bitfs/fuse_ops.h: FUSE callback signatures and private data structures.
  2. src/fuse_main.c: The entry point (main) that parses arguments and invokes fuse_main.
  3. src/fuse_callbacks.c: Detailed implementation of the fuse_operations struct.
  4. src/fuse_helpers.c: Path validation and errno translation utilities.
  5. tests/test_mount.sh: Integration script using standard Unix tools against a live mount.

COMPLETE DATA MODEL

FUSE allows us to pass a "Private Data" pointer to every callback. We use this to provide the block device context to the stateless FUSE handlers.

1. The FUSE Private Context (bitfs_fuse_context_t)

This structure is initialized at startup and passed into fuse_main.

c
typedef struct {
    bitfs_block_device_t *dev;    // Open handle to the disk image (from M1)
    bitfs_superblock_t *sb;       // Cached copy of the superblock (from M1)
    pthread_mutex_t global_lock;  // The Big Kernel Lock (BKL) for BitFS
    char *image_path;             // Path to the .img file on the host FS
} bitfs_fuse_context_t;

2. The Stat Bridge (Hardware Soul: Metadata Alignment)

When the kernel calls getattr, we must fill the struct stat. This requires precise mapping of BitFS's 128-byte inode fields to the OS-specific stat structure.

BitFS Inode Field POSIX Stat Field Bit-Level Note
mode st_mode Includes file type (S_IFREG/S_IFDIR) and 9-bit permissions.
nlink st_nlink Must be accurate for ls to report directory sub-item counts.
size st_size 64-bit on modern systems; BitFS uses 32-bit (4GB limit).
ino st_ino The unique index node number.
atime/mtime/ctime st_atim/mtim/ctim BitFS stores seconds; convert to struct timespec.

3. FUSE File Info (fuse_file_info)

The fuse_file_info struct is passed by the kernel to track open file handles.

c
struct fuse_file_info {
    int flags;            // O_RDONLY, O_WRONLY, O_APPEND, etc.
    unsigned long fh;     // We can store the Inode ID here to avoid re-lookups.
    int writepage;        // Internal FUSE use.
    unsigned int direct_io : 1; // If 1, bypasses host OS page cache.
    // ... other fields ...
};

Figure 1: The Context Bridge. bitfs_fuse_context_t acting as the anchor for all callback functions, linking libfuse threads to the underlying block device.

INTERFACE CONTRACTS

All functions follow the FUSE convention: return 0 for success or -errno (e.g., -EACCES) for failure.

1. Metadata: getattr

Signature: int bitfs_fuse_getattr(const char *path, struct stat *stbuf, struct fuse_file_info *fi)

  • Input: Absolute path string.
  • Logic:
    1. Lock global mutex.
    2. Call bitfs_resolve_path(path) to get Inode ID.
    3. Call bitfs_inode_read() for that ID.
    4. Fill stbuf using the mapping table above.
    5. Unlock global mutex.
  • Error: Return -ENOENT if path resolution fails.

2. Directory Listing: readdir

Signature: int bitfs_fuse_readdir(const char *path, void *buf, fuse_fill_dir_t filler, off_t offset, struct fuse_file_info *fi)

  • Logic:
    1. Resolve path to Inode. Verify S_ISDIR.
    2. Iterate through directory data blocks (using logic from M3).
    3. For every valid dirent, call filler(buf, entry->name, &st, 0, 0).
    4. filler handles the buffer management for the kernel.

3. File I/O: read & write

Signature: int bitfs_fuse_read(const char *path, char *buf, size_t size, off_t offset, struct fuse_file_info *fi)

  • Constraint: Use fi->fh (if set during open) to skip path resolution, drastically reducing I/O latency.
  • Logic: Call bitfs_read(fi->fh, buf, size, offset).

Signature: int bitfs_fuse_write(const char *path, const char *buf, size_t size, off_t offset, struct fuse_file_info *fi)

  • Constraint: Must update mtime and size in the inode and write back to disk.
  • Logic: Call bitfs_write(fi->fh, buf, size, offset).

ALGORITHM SPECIFICATION: The FUSE Request Lifecycle

Because FUSE is multi-threaded, the implementation must handle concurrent requests for the same path.

Procedure: Path-to-Inode-with-Locking

  1. Kernel sends LOOKUP(parent_inode_id, "filename").
  2. FUSE lookup callback (or getattr implementation) is invoked.
  3. Step 1: Acquire Global Mutex.
    • Systems Soul: This is a "Coarse-Grained" lock. It ensures only one thread is scanning the Inode Table or Bitmaps at a time.
  4. Step 2: String Tokenization.
    • If path is /a/b/c, split into ["a", "b", "c"].
  5. Step 3: Iterative Search.
    • Start at root Inode (1).
    • For each token, read the directory blocks, find the dirent, extract the Inode ID.
    • Check permissions (mode & S_IRUSR) at each step.
  6. Step 4: Result Cache (Optional but Recommended).
    • Store the resulting Inode ID in the fuse_file_info->fh if this is part of an open() call.
  7. Step 5: Release Global Mutex.

Algorithm: Directory Entry "filler" Pattern When implementing readdir, FUSE uses a "Pull-style" generator pattern.

  1. Initialize a struct stat with zeros.
  2. In your directory scan loop:
    • st.st_ino = dirent->inode_num;
    • st.st_mode = (dirent->file_type == BITFS_FT_DIR) ? S_IFDIR : S_IFREG;
    • Call filler(buf, dirent->name, &st, 0, 0).
    • If filler returns 1, the kernel buffer is full. Stop and return 0.

Figure 2: The Multi-Threaded Request Flow. Showing how libfuse worker threads contend for the BitFS Global Mutex before accessing the Inode Table.

ERROR HANDLING MATRIX

Error Detected By Recovery User-Visible?
E_KERNEL_COMM_ERROR fuse_main Usually results in a "Transport endpoint is not connected" error on the host. Requires remount. Yes
E_DEADLOCK Locking logic Caused by acquiring the Global Mutex twice in the same thread. Use pthread_mutex_trylock for debugging or ensure strict single-acquisition. No (Process Hangs)
ENOENT resolve_path Return -ENOENT. Kernel tells user "No such file or directory". Yes
ENOTEMPTY rmdir Return if directory data blocks contain entries other than . and ... Yes
EIO Block Layer If device_read fails, return -EIO. Indicates disk corruption. Yes

CONCURRENCY SPECIFICATION

BitFS uses a Threaded-Safe Coarse Locking Strategy.

  1. The Global Mutex: A single pthread_mutex_t protects the entire filesystem state.
  2. Lock Order:
    • Always: pthread_mutex_lock(&ctx->global_lock) at the start of every FUSE callback.
    • Always: pthread_mutex_unlock(&ctx->global_lock) before returning from the callback.
  3. Recursive Safety: Functions called within the callbacks (like bitfs_read) must NOT attempt to acquire the lock again, or a deadlock will occur.
  4. FUSE Multithreading: We will run fuse_main without the -s (single-threaded) flag to allow multiple cat or ls requests to be queued, though they will serialize on the Global Mutex.

HARDWARE SOUL: CONTEXT SWITCHES

Every call to bitfs_fuse_read involves a User-to-Kernel-to-User transition.

  • The Cost: A context switch costs ~5-10 microseconds.
  • The Impact: If a user app reads 1 byte at a time, the FUSE overhead will be 10,000x slower than the actual data copy.
  • Optimization: libfuse handles this by batching reads into 128KB chunks (the max_read setting). Ensure your bitfs_read slicing logic (M4) is efficient enough to handle these large requests in a single lock acquisition to minimize the "Instruction Gap."

Figure 3: Sequence Diagram. Application -> Syscall -> VFS -> FUSE Kernel -> BitFS Daemon -> Block Layer. Visualizing the round-trip latency.

MODULE CHARTER: Write-Ahead Journaling and Crash Recovery (filesystem-m4)

The Write-Ahead Journaling module provides BitFS with the property of crash consistency. It implements a metadata-only journaling system (often called "physical journaling" for metadata) where all structural changes—inode updates, bitmap toggles, and directory entry modifications—are recorded in a sequential, circular log before being applied to the primary filesystem structures. This module ensures that even if the system loses power or crashes mid-operation, the filesystem can be restored to a consistent state by replaying committed transactions or discarding incomplete ones.

What it DOES:

  • Implements a Circular Buffer log within a dedicated disk region.
  • Provides a Transaction API (begin, log, commit) that wraps metadata operations.
  • Enforces Write Barriers using fdatasync() to ensure the journal is durable before the main filesystem is modified.
  • Performs Atomic Recovery on mount by scanning the journal and replaying valid transactions.
  • Implements Checksumming to detect "torn writes" caused by power failure during a sector write.

What it does NOT do:

  • Journal actual file data (it uses "Ordered" mode where data is written before metadata is committed).
  • Handle background threads for checkpointing (checkpointing is synchronous when the journal fills).
  • Perform full filesystem checks (fsck) beyond the journal region.

Invariants:

  • A transaction is considered valid only if its commit block is successfully written and flushed to disk.
  • No metadata block may be written to its primary location until the corresponding journal entry is flushed.
  • The journal tail must never overwrite the head until a checkpoint has occurred.

FILE STRUCTURE

The journaling system is built as a standalone subsystem that hooks into the existing block and inode layers.

  1. include/bitfs/journal.h: On-disk structures for journal headers, descriptors, and tags.
  2. src/journal.c: Transaction management logic and circular buffer offsets.
  3. src/recovery.c: The replay engine used during filesystem mounting.
  4. tests/test_journal_stress.c: Crash simulation testing using fork() and state verification.

COMPLETE DATA MODEL

The journal region (defined in the Superblock) is treated as a linear sequence of blocks. Block 0 of this region is the Journal Header.

1. Journal Header (bitfs_journal_header_t)

Stored at sb->journal_start. Occupies 1 full block (4KB).

Offset Size Type Name Description
0x00 4 uint32_t magic Journal Magic: 0x4A524E4C ("JRNL").
0x04 4 uint32_t block_size Fixed at 4096.
0x08 4 uint32_t total_blocks Total size of journal region (inc. header).
0x0C 4 uint32_t head Block offset of oldest active transaction.
0x10 4 uint32_t tail Block offset where next transaction starts.
0x14 4 uint32_t sequence_num Incrementing ID for transaction ordering.
0x18 4072 uint8_t[] padding Reserved.

2. The Transaction Format

A transaction consists of:

  1. One Descriptor Block: Contains a list of target physical block IDs.
  2. N Data Blocks: The actual 4KB content of the metadata blocks being changed.
  3. One Commit Block: A small record marking the transaction as complete.

Descriptor Block (bitfs_journal_desc_t)

Offset Size Type Name Description
0x00 4 uint32_t magic 0x44455343 ("DESC").
0x04 4 uint32_t seq Matches header sequence_num.
0x08 4 uint32_t num_blocks Number of data blocks following this.
0x0C 4084 uint32_t[] block_ids Array of physical block IDs for the data.

Commit Block (bitfs_journal_commit_t)

Offset Size Type Name Description
0x00 4 uint32_t magic 0x434D4954 ("CMIT").
0x04 4 uint32_t seq Matches descriptor seq.
0x08 4 uint32_t checksum CRC32 of all data blocks in this TX.
0x0C 4084 uint8_t[] padding Reserved.

Figure 1: Circular Journal Layout. [Header] -> [DESC] [DATA...] [CMIT] -> [DESC] [DATA...] [CMIT] -> (Head/Tail wrap).

INTERFACE CONTRACTS

1. The Transaction API

Function: int bitfs_journal_begin(bitfs_journal_t* j, uint32_t* out_tx_id)

  • Logic: Increments sequence_num. Verifies that the journal has enough space (at least 10 blocks) between tail and head.
  • Return: 0 or E_JOURNAL_FULL.

Function: int bitfs_journal_log(bitfs_journal_t* j, uint32_t physical_block_num, const void* data)

  • Input: The block number in the main FS and the 4KB dirty content.
  • Logic: Buffers the block ID in the bitfs_journal_desc_t and writes the data to the journal's tail block on disk. Advances tail.

Function: int bitfs_journal_commit(bitfs_journal_t* j)

  • Logic:
    1. Writes the bitfs_journal_desc_t block to the journal.
    2. Writes the bitfs_journal_commit_t block.
    3. Crucial: Calls fdatasync(dev->fd) to ensure the journal is durable.
    4. Updates the Journal Header's tail on disk.

2. Recovery Engine

Function: int bitfs_recover(bitfs_block_device_t* dev)

  • Logic: Invoked during mount. Scans from head to tail.
  • Constraint: If a transaction is found without a matching commit or with a bad checksum, scanning stops.

ALGORITHM SPECIFICATION

1. The Atomic Update Sequence (The Write Barrier)

To move a file safely, the code must execute these steps in strict order:

Procedure:

  1. bitfs_journal_begin(): Reserve a new sequence number.
  2. bitfs_journal_log(inode_block_id, new_inode_data): Write "prophecy" to journal.
  3. bitfs_journal_log(dir_block_id, new_dir_data): Write "prophecy" to journal.
  4. bitfs_journal_commit():
    • Write descriptor and commit blocks to journal.
    • fdatasync(): The Write Barrier. Hardware must confirm these are on persistent media.
  5. Apply Phase:
    • Write new_inode_data to its primary inode_block_id.
    • Write new_dir_data to its primary dir_block_id.
  6. Checkpoint: (Occurs later) Update Journal Header head to reflect that these transactions are now safely in their primary home.

Figure 2: Transaction Timeline. T1: Log to Journal -> T2: Commit Record -> T3: fdatasync() -> T4: Apply to Main FS.

2. The Checkpoint & Wrap-around

The journal is finite. When tail approaches head, we must "Checkpoint."

Procedure:

  1. Call fdatasync() on the main block device. This ensures all "Applied" changes (Step 5 above) are physically on the disk.
  2. Update the Journal Header: header->head = header->tail.
  3. Reset sequence_num if desired, or just continue.
  4. Write the Header back to the disk.
  5. This clears the journal, allowing tail to wrap back to Block 1 of the region.

3. Recovery Replay (Idempotent Logic)

If the system crashes, the Apply Phase may have been interrupted.

Procedure:

  1. Read Journal Header. Set curr = header->head.
  2. Loop:
    • Read block at curr as a bitfs_journal_desc_t.
    • If Magic != DESC or Sequence != expected: Break.
    • Look ahead num_blocks + 1 to find bitfs_journal_commit_t.
    • If Commit Magic != CMIT or Sequence mismatch: Break.
    • Verify Checksum: Calculate CRC32 of the num_blocks following the descriptor. If mismatch: Break.
    • Replay: For i = 0 to num_blocks - 1:
      • data = journal_region[curr + 1 + i]
      • target = descriptor->block_ids[i]
      • device_write_block(dev, target, data)
    • curr += num_blocks + 2.
  3. Once loop ends: header->head = header->tail = curr. Update Header on disk.

Figure 3: Recovery Replay Algorithm. Validating the transaction "envelope" before applying the payload.

ERROR HANDLING MATRIX

Error Detected By Recovery User-Visible?
E_JOURNAL_FULL journal_begin Trigger an immediate Checkpoint. If still full, return error. Yes (I/O Error)
E_CHECKSUM_MISMATCH bitfs_recover Stop replay. This transaction and all subsequent ones are discarded. No
E_TORN_COMMIT bitfs_recover If Commit block is partial (Magic found but seq wrong), discard. No
E_BARRIER_FAILURE fdatasync Filesystem must enter Read-Only mode immediately. Yes

IMPLEMENTATION SEQUENCE

Phase 1: Journal Circular Buffer and Header (3-4 Hours)

Goal: Initialize the journal region and handle pointer wrapping.

  1. Implement bitfs_journal_init (calculates offsets).
  2. Implement header read/write functions.
  3. Checkpoint: Run mkfs. Manually write 10 blocks to the journal region and verify that tail increments and wraps correctly from the end of the region back to the start.

Phase 2: Transaction API (Begin/Commit/Log) (5-7 Hours)

Goal: Wrap existing metadata operations in transactions.

  1. Implement journal_begin, journal_log_block, and journal_commit.
  2. Modify inode_write and dir_add_entry to use the journal.
    • Note: These functions now take a journal_t* pointer.
  3. Checkpoint: Enable journaling. Run a script that creates 10 files. Use hexdump on the journal region of the disk image to verify that DESC and CMIT blocks are present and contain the correct physical block IDs.

Phase 3: Recovery Logic and Replay Algorithm (4-5 Hours)

Goal: Resilience against crashes.

  1. Implement the replay logic in bitfs_recover.
  2. Hook bitfs_recover into the start of the mount process.
  3. Checkpoint (The "Pull the Plug" Test):
    • Start a process that creates files.
    • Use kill -9 on the BitFS process.
    • Crucial: Manually corrupt the primary inode table for a file that was just being created (using dd), but leave the journal intact.
    • Mount the filesystem.
    • Verify that the recovery logic restores the inode table from the journaled copy.

CONCURRENCY SPECIFICATION

Journaling requires strict serialization.

  1. The Journal Lock: A single mutex journal_lock must be held for the duration of a transaction (from begin to commit).
  2. Ordered Data Writes: To prevent "garbage data" (where metadata points to a block containing old data), BitFS must follow this order:
    • Write file DATA blocks to disk.
    • fdatasync() the data blocks.
    • journal_begin() for the metadata changes.
    • This ensures the data is safe before the "prophecy" (metadata) is even written to the journal.

HARDWARE SOUL: TORN WRITES & BARRIERS

In the context of the Hardware Soul, journaling is a battle against the disk controller's desire to reorder.

  • The Write Barrier: Without fdatasync(), the disk might write the Inode Table update before the Journal Commit. If a crash occurs between them, the journal recovery will find no commit and do nothing, leaving the Inode Table with partial, un-journaled changes.
  • Sector Atomicity: Most disks guarantee that a 512-byte sector write is atomic. However, our blocks are 4096 bytes (8 sectors). A "Torn Write" happens when power fails after writing 4 sectors but before the 8th.
  • The Checksum Solution: By storing a CRC32 of the data blocks in the commit block, we detect torn writes. If only 4 sectors were written, the CRC32 will fail, and we will safely ignore the corrupt transaction.

TEST SPECIFICATION

1. The Crash Recovery Consistency Test

  • Action: Open a transaction, log 3 blocks, then crash the process before the commit block is written.
  • Expected: On remount, the journal tail should be rolled back to the last valid commit. The 3 blocks should NOT be replayed.

2. The Checkpoint Wrap Test

  • Action: Create a very small journal (e.g., 20 blocks). Create 100 files.
  • Expected: The system must trigger multiple checkpoints. Verify that head and tail move around the circular buffer without ever overlapping.

3. The Multi-Block Transaction Test

  • Action: Perform an operation that touches multiple metadata regions (e.g., rename across directories: touches 2 dir blocks, 1 inode, 1 bitmap).
  • Expected: A single DESC block contains all 4 physical IDs. Replay must apply all 4 or none.

PERFORMANCE TARGETS

Operation Target How to Measure
Recovery Time < 100ms Measure mount time after a dirty unmount with a full journal.
Write Amplification 2.0x (Metadata) Count physical writes per mkdir vs. non-journaled mode.
Journal Throughput > 100MB/s Sequential write speed to the journal region.

Project Structure: Filesystem Implementation (BitFS)

Directory Tree

markdown
bitfs-project/
├── include/
│   └── bitfs/
│       ├── blocks.h         # Block device API (M1)
│       ├── layout.h         # Superblock & disk geometry (M1)

title: 🎯 Project Charter: BitFS Journaled Filesystem
markdown:
# 🎯 Project Charter: BitFS Journaled Filesystem

## What You Are Building
A fully functional, inode-based filesystem (BitFS) implemented in user-space using the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) protocol. You will build a system that manages a raw disk image as a grid of 4KB blocks, featuring an on-disk layout with superblocks, bitmaps for allocation, an inode table for metadata, and a write-ahead journal for crash consistency. By the end, you will have a filesystem daemon that can be mounted as a real directory on Linux or macOS, allowing you to use standard commands like `ls`, `mkdir`, and `cp` on your custom storage engine.

## Why This Project Exists
Most developers interact with files as abstract byte-streams, treating the underlying storage as a black box. Building a filesystem from scratch exposes the rigid physical reality of block-level I/O, the mathematical elegance of indirection trees (inodes), and the complex engineering required to ensure data integrity during power failures. This project transforms you from a consumer of storage APIs into an architect who understands how databases and operating systems manage persistence at the bit level.

## What You Will Be Able to Do When Done
- **Format Raw Storage:** Create a `mkfs` tool that initializes a structured filesystem geometry on any binary file.
- **Implement Multi-level Indirection:** Design pointer trees (direct, single-indirect, and double-indirect) to support files ranging from bytes to gigabytes.
- **Manage Hierarchical Namespaces:** Build directory traversal engines that resolve string paths like `/var/log/syslog` into internal inode identifiers.
- **Interface with the Kernel:** Use FUSE to bridge your user-space code with the Virtual File System (VFS) layer of the operating system.
- **Engineered Crash Resilience:** Implement a Write-Ahead Journal (WAL) that can replay or discard transactions to recover a corrupted filesystem after a simulated crash.

## Final Deliverable
A robust C, Rust, or Go implementation consisting of ~3,000–5,000 lines of code. The artifact includes a `mkfs.bitfs` formatting utility and a `bitfsd` daemon. The finished project will successfully pass a "Crash Test": creating files during a forced process kill and automatically recovering to a consistent state upon remounting, verified by a custom `fsck` integrity check.

## Is This Project For You?
**You should start this if you:**
- Are comfortable with manual memory management and pointer arithmetic (especially if using C).
- Understand bitwise operations (AND, OR, XOR, Shifting) for bitmap manipulation.
- Have a basic grasp of POSIX file I/O (open, read, write, lseek).
- Want to move from "Application Developer" to "Systems Engineer."

**Come back after you've learned:**
- [C Struct Packing and Alignment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_structure_alignment) — crucial for on-disk serialization.
- [The Basics of Mutexes and Threading](https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/thread) — FUSE is multi-threaded by default.

## Estimated Effort
| Phase | Time |
|-------|------|
| Block Layer and mkfs | ~12 hours |
| Inode Management & Indirection | ~12 hours |
| Directory Operations & Path Resolution | ~12 hours |
| File Read/Write & Slicing Logic | ~12 hours |
| FUSE Integration | ~12 hours |
| Write-Ahead Journaling & Recovery | ~14 hours |
| **Total** | **~74 hours** |

## Definition of Done
The project is complete when:
- The filesystem mounts successfully via FUSE and is visible in the output of the `mount` command.
- Standard Unix utilities (`ls -la`, `mkdir`, `rm`, `cat`, `cp`) work on the mount point without errors.
- A 100MB file can be copied into the filesystem and its `md5sum` remains identical to the original.
- The filesystem process can be killed (`kill -9`) mid-write, and the subsequent mount triggers a journal replay that leaves the metadata in a consistent state.
- All allocated blocks and inodes are correctly tracked; running the custom `fsck` tool on a clean unmount reports zero leaked blocks and zero orphaned inodes.

---

# 📚 Before You Read This: Prerequisites & Further Reading

> **Read these first.** The Atlas assumes you are familiar with the foundations below.
> Resources are ordered by when you should encounter them — some before you start, some at specific milestones.

---

### 🏗️ Topic: Foundational File System Architecture
**Read BEFORE starting Milestone 1.** These resources establish the mental model of how a disk is partitioned into metadata and data regions.

*   **Paper**: [The UNIX Time-Sharing System](https://www.bell-labs.com/usr/dmr/www/cacm.pdf) (Ritchie & Thompson, 1974). 
*   **Code**: [xv6-riscv / kernel / fs.c](https://github.com/mit-pdos/xv6-riscv/blob/f5b93936a7a518c0a501a355474347c439169641/kernel/fs.c) — Specifically the block allocation and inode read/write logic.
*   **Best Explanation**: [Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces (OSTEP)](https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~remzi/OSTEP/file-implementation.pdf), Chapter 40: "File System Implementation."
*   **Why**: It provides the simplest possible pedagogical overview of the "Fixed-Size Array" model for inodes and bitmaps used in BitFS.
*   **Pedagogical Timing**: Read before Milestone 1 to understand why we carve the disk into a Superblock, Bitmap, and Inode Table.

---

### 🧬 Topic: Inodes & Multi-Level Indirection
**Read after Milestone 1, before Milestone 2.** Required for implementing the pointer tree.

*   **Paper**: [The Design and Implementation of the 4.4BSD Operating System](https://archive.org/details/designimplementa0000mcku) (McKusick et al., 1996) — Section 7.2 on Inodes.
*   **Code**: [Linux Kernel / fs / ext2 / inode.c](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/fs/ext2/inode.c) — Look at `ext2_get_branch` for the actual logic of traversing indirect blocks.
*   **Best Explanation**: [The Linux Documentation Project: The Inode](https://tldp.org/LDP/tlk/fs/filesystem.html#inode).
*   **Why**: This is the industry-standard method for supporting multi-gigabyte files while keeping the inode structure small and fixed-size.
*   **Pedagogical Timing**: Read after you finish the Block Layer (MS1) but before writing the `bmap` function (MS2).
